employee_and_department_information.depa.answer: Cook County Department of Facilities Management

previous_disciplinary_actions.previous_d.answer: Verbal counseling documented in supervisor’s log

Late arrival to shift on three occasions within a four-week period (ranging from 12 to 22 minutes late) without notifying supervisor in advance, contrary to Facilities Management attendance expectations and the departmental call‑in procedure.

Failure to complete assigned preventive maintenance checklist for the 5th floor air handling unit at the George N. Leighton Criminal Courthouse and failure to document status in the computerized maintenance management system (CMMS), resulting in an unplanned outage and tenant complaints regarding temperature control.

Leaving assigned work area without authorization for approximately 55 minutes; using a County vehicle for a non‑work‑related detour to a private residence while on duty; and failure to respond promptly to radio calls from the control center, in violation of departmental work rules governing time on duty, vehicle use, and responsiveness.

basis_for_recommended_action.basis_for_r.answer:
Summary of Employee and Position
Marcus D. Ellison has been employed with the Cook County Department of Facilities Management since March 2019 as a Building Maintenance Engineer II assigned primarily to the George N. Leighton Criminal Courthouse and, on occasion, to nearby County facilities as needed. In this role, he is responsible for monitoring and maintaining building mechanical systems, including boilers, chillers, pumps, air handling units, and associated controls, as well as responding to work orders and ensuring that building conditions remain safe for staff, detainees, court personnel, and members of the public.

The recommended disciplinary action is termination of employment based on repeated performance deficiencies, misconduct relating to safety protocols, and dishonesty during an internal investigation. The incidents outlined below demonstrate a pattern of behavior that has not improved despite progressive discipline.

Description of the Current Incident(s)
On 2026-01-29, at approximately 7:40 a.m., Mr. Ellison began his scheduled day shift (7:30 a.m.–3:30 p.m.) at the George N. Leighton Criminal Courthouse. At 8:05 a.m., the Facilities Control Center received a temperature alarm indicating an abnormal rise in temperature in Mechanical Room 3B, which houses electrical panels, pumps, and chemical storage used for the closed-loop water treatment system.

Per standard operating procedures, the on-duty engineer is required to respond immediately to such alarms, assess the situation, and, if necessary, initiate lockout/tagout and notify the supervisor. Video footage and badge access logs confirm that Mr. Ellison entered Mechanical Room 3B at 8:11 a.m. and remained there alone for approximately 18 minutes.

During this period, Mr. Ellison attempted to troubleshoot a recirculation pump that had tripped offline and was emitting a noticeable burning odor. Instead of following the posted lockout/tagout procedure and de-energizing the equipment at the disconnect switch before working near the pump housing, Mr. Ellison opened the pump access panel while the unit remained energized. He then used a metal screwdriver to attempt to dislodge debris from the pump area and reset the thermal overload manually.

At approximately 8:24 a.m., the pump sparked and a small electrical arc occurred. Although the arc did not result in serious injury, Mr. Ellison jerked back and struck a nearby chemical storage shelf, dislodging several containers of corrosion inhibitor and biocide solution. Two containers fell to the floor and one cracked open, releasing liquid onto the floor. The spill triggered the room’s chemical vapor sensor at 8:26 a.m., which automatically notified both the Facilities Control Center and building security.

Instead of immediately reporting the spill and evacuating the mechanical room as required by the posted Hazardous Materials Response Procedure, Mr. Ellison attempted to clean the spill himself using shop towels and a standard mop bucket without donning appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) or consulting the Safety Data Sheets (SDS) located just inside the mechanical room. Camera footage shows that he did not put on chemical-resistant gloves, goggles, or a respirator. He also did not place any signage or barriers to prevent others from entering the area.

At approximately 8:33 a.m., Security Officer Thomas Nguyen arrived at Mechanical Room 3B in response to the chemical alarm. Officer Nguyen observed a strong chemical odor and found Mr. Ellison inside the room, visibly coughing and wiping the floor. Officer Nguyen ordered Mr. Ellison to exit the room and radioed the Control Center to report a chemical spill. Only at that point was the area properly isolated and the County’s contracted hazardous materials response vendor notified.

The spill itself was ultimately contained by 9:05 a.m., but the mechanical room was taken offline for ventilation and decontamination. As a result, access to that section of the building was restricted for several hours, and one court call was delayed due to the temporary closure of an adjacent corridor. Additionally, Mr. Ellison was transported to Employee Health for evaluation due to potential chemical exposure and minor irritation to his eyes and upper respiratory tract.

Failure to Follow Safety Protocols
The Department of Facilities Management has clear, written safety procedures that are regularly reviewed with staff and posted in relevant work areas. Mr. Ellison completed mandatory safety training on 2025-09-14, which included modules on lockout/tagout, chemical handling, and emergency response procedures. He signed an acknowledgment form confirming that he had received, reviewed, and understood these procedures.

Despite this, the investigation determined that Mr. Ellison failed to:

1. De-energize equipment prior to inspection or maintenance
   - The lockout/tagout procedure posted inside Mechanical Room 3B and in the Facilities Safety Manual requires that all power sources be disconnected and locked out before opening any access panels or working near moving parts.
   - Security video and equipment logs confirm that no lockout/tagout devices were applied to the pump’s disconnect switch prior to Mr. Ellison opening the access panel.
   - Mr. Ellison admitted during the investigatory interview that he believed he could “just take a quick look” without shutting the pump down fully because he was “trying not to cause more alarms.”

2. Properly respond to a chemical spill
   - The Hazardous Materials Response Procedure, which is posted near the entrance of Mechanical Room 3B and covered in annual refresher training, requires staff to:
     • Immediately stop work and evacuate the area if a spill occurs.
     • Notify the Control Center and supervisor without delay.
     • Avoid attempting cleanup unless trained and equipped with appropriate PPE.
   - Mr. Ellison remained in the room attempting to clean the spill for approximately seven minutes before security arrived, without contacting the Control Center or his supervisor.
   - He did not utilize the spill kit located approximately 15 feet from where the containers fell, nor did he review the SDS binder clearly mounted on the wall next to the entrance.

3. Use required personal protective equipment (PPE)
   - Mr. Ellison had access to chemical-resistant gloves, safety goggles, and respirators in the PPE cabinet located in the same mechanical room.
   - Video evidence shows he continued working in the contaminated area wearing only his standard work uniform, steel-toe boots, and a cloth face covering, despite visible vapors and a strong odor.

These failures represent serious violations of both departmental policy and basic workplace safety practices, and they placed Mr. Ellison, security staff, and potentially other County employees at unnecessary risk.

Dishonesty and Misrepresentation During the Investigation
In addition to the underlying safety violations, Mr. Ellison was not fully truthful during the subsequent internal investigation conducted by Facilities Management and the Bureau of Human Resources.

During his initial written incident report submitted on 2026-01-30, Mr. Ellison stated that:

- He had “followed all lockout/tagout procedures” before inspecting the pump; and
- He had “immediately exited the room and called the Control Center” upon noticing the chemical spill.

However, these statements are directly contradicted by:

- Security camera footage, which clearly shows Mr. Ellison opening the pump panel without applying any lockout devices, and remaining in the room after the containers fell, attempting to mop up the spill.
- Radio logs and phone records, which show that the first report of the chemical alarm came from the automated monitoring system and was followed by a call from Security Officer Nguyen, not Mr. Ellison.

When confronted with the discrepancies during a follow-up interview on 2026-02-05, with union representation present, Mr. Ellison initially maintained that he had shut down the pump at the disconnect switch. Only after being advised that video footage and electrical logs would be reviewed did he acknowledge that he had not used a lockout device and that he “might have overstated” his compliance with procedure in his written report.

This sequence of events demonstrates not only poor judgment in the moment, but a subsequent attempt to minimize or misrepresent his actions. Dishonesty during an internal investigation undermines management’s ability to rely on the employee’s statements and constitutes a separate, serious offense under departmental work rules.

Impact on Operations and Safety
While it is fortunate that there were no serious injuries or long-term damage to County property, the incident had several tangible negative impacts:

- Mechanical Room Downtime: Mechanical Room 3B was out of service for approximately four hours for cleanup and ventilation, limiting access to certain valves and controls.
- Court Operations: One morning court call was delayed approximately 35 minutes due to limited access to a nearby corridor while the area was secured and checked for air quality.
- Staff Exposure Risk: Mr. Ellison and Security Officer Nguyen were both potentially exposed to chemical vapors due to the initial uncontrolled spill and lack of immediate evacuation.
- Additional Costs: The County incurred unplanned costs associated with the emergency response from the hazardous materials vendor and additional air quality testing.

Given the highly sensitive nature of courthouse operations and the requirement to maintain safe and orderly conditions for the judiciary, staff, detainees, and the public, adherence to safety protocols is non-negotiable. Mr. Ellison’s actions demonstrated a disregard for those protocols, even though he had been trained and previously counseled on the importance of following procedures.

Prior Disciplinary History and Progressive Discipline
Mr. Ellison has a documented history of performance and conduct issues that have previously been addressed through progressive discipline:

1. Verbal Counseling – 2024-06-18
   - Reason: Repeated tardiness (three late arrivals within four weeks) without advance notice to his supervisor.
   - Action Taken: Verbal counseling documented in the supervisor’s log. Mr. Ellison was reminded of the attendance policy and the requirement to call in at least 30 minutes before his scheduled start time if delayed.

2. Written Warning – 2024-11-04
   - Reason: Failure to complete a preventive maintenance checklist for the 5th floor air handling unit and failure to document status in the CMMS, resulting in an unplanned equipment outage and tenant complaints.
   - Action Taken: Written warning issued, emphasizing the importance of completing all assigned preventive maintenance tasks and accurately documenting work. Mr. Ellison was advised that further performance issues could result in more serious discipline.

3. Three-Day Suspension Without Pay – 2025-05-12
   - Reason: Leaving his assigned work area without authorization for nearly an hour, taking a County vehicle on a non-work-related detour to a private residence, and failing to respond promptly to radio calls.
   - Action Taken: Three-day suspension, with a clear directive that misuse of County time and property and failure to respond to operational needs would not be tolerated. Mr. Ellison was explicitly advised that any further serious misconduct could lead to termination.

Despite these prior interventions, Mr. Ellison’s conduct has not improved to the level expected of a Building Maintenance Engineer II. The current incident is more serious in nature than prior issues because it involves safety violations and dishonesty, rather than solely attendance or performance deficiencies.

Reason for Recommended Termination Rather Than Further Suspension
In determining the appropriate level of discipline, management considered the following factors:

- Severity of the Safety Violations: Mr. Ellison’s failure to follow lockout/tagout procedures and chemical spill response protocols created a risk of serious injury to himself and others. Safety procedures are fundamental to the operation of mechanical systems in a public facility and are repeatedly emphasized in training.

- Dishonesty During the Investigation: Mr. Ellison’s inaccurate written report and initial insistence that he had followed procedures, in the face of contradictory evidence, erode management’s trust in his judgment and reliability. Honesty is essential when investigating safety-related incidents so that corrective actions can be properly identified.

- Prior Progressive Discipline: Mr. Ellison has already received verbal counseling, a written warning, and a suspension for prior issues related to attendance, performance, and misuse of County property and time. He was previously warned that additional, serious misconduct could result in termination.

- Position Responsibilities: As a Building Maintenance Engineer II, Mr. Ellison is expected to exercise independent judgment and work with minimal supervision, particularly during off-hours and emergency conditions. His demonstrated inability or unwillingness to follow established safety procedures undermines confidence in his ability to perform these duties safely.

- Potential Liability and Risk: If similar behavior were to recur, the County could face significant liability in the event of an injury, chemical exposure, equipment damage, or disruption to court operations. Retaining an employee who has engaged in this pattern of conduct and dishonesty would expose the County to unnecessary risk.

After weighing these factors, management determined that further suspension would not be sufficient to correct behavior that appears to be rooted in poor judgment, disregard for established procedures, and lack of candor. Termination is therefore recommended as the appropriate and necessary response.

Mitigating and Aggravating Factors Considered
Management did consider several potential mitigating factors:

- Length of Service: Mr. Ellison has been with the County for approximately seven years and, during some periods, has performed his duties at a satisfactory level.
- Personal Circumstances: During the investigatory interview, Mr. Ellison mentioned that he has been under stress due to caring for an elderly parent and managing childcare responsibilities. While these circumstances may help explain some of the attendance issues in 2024, they do not justify safety violations or dishonesty related to the current incident.
- Cooperation at Later Stages: After being confronted with the evidence, Mr. Ellison did ultimately acknowledge that he had not followed lockout/tagout procedures as required.

However, several aggravating factors outweighed these mitigating considerations:

- Repetition of Misconduct Despite Prior Discipline: Mr. Ellison has continued to engage in rule violations even after being clearly warned and suspended.
- Nature of the Current Misconduct: The current incident involves safety, hazardous materials, and dishonesty, which are more serious than prior attendance and performance issues.
- Impact on Operational Trust: Supervisors must be able to rely on building engineers to accurately report incidents and adhere to safety procedures when working alone. Mr. Ellison’s actions have undermined that trust.

Conclusion
Based on the totality of the circumstances, including the detailed findings of the internal investigation, the documented prior disciplinary history, and the serious nature of the current safety violations and dishonesty, the Department of Facilities Management recommends termination of Marcus D. Ellison’s employment.

This recommendation is made in accordance with Cook County’s progressive discipline policy, applicable collective bargaining agreements, and the County’s obligation to maintain a safe, reliable, and trustworthy workforce in a high‑risk, high‑traffic public facility. Lesser forms of discipline have been tried and have not resulted in sustained improvement. Termination is therefore warranted and necessary to protect the interests of the County and the safety of its employees and the public.
